sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 77, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: ** Level 1 contains a grid with a vertical dotted white line dividing it into left (dark gray background) and right (black background) sections. On the left: a gray rectangular block, a blue square, a yellow square, a multicolored L-shaped structure (red, gray, green). On the right: a red rectangular shape and a blue rectangular shape. The goal is likely to manipulate objects to clear the level, possibly by matching or moving shapes across the divider.
- Goal model: ** Clear level 1 by solving a puzzle involving the colored shapes, likely requiring interaction with the objects.
- Action model: ** Available actions are directional movement (UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT) and MOUSE clicks. The MOUSE action appears most promising for interacting with specific objects.
- Recent findings: ** The board is split by a vertical divider with distinct colored objects on each side. The left side has a complex L-shaped structure and individual colored squares, while the right has larger colored rectangles. The previous MOUSE clicks at (48, 19), (20, 21), (24, 20), (14, 36), (49, 36), (49, 20), (49, 13), (23, 22), (20, 21), (15, 32), (49, 47), (40, 32), (24, 21), (48, 18), (23, 22), (21, 21), (15, 36), (49, 47), (17, 19), (18, 22), (23, 22), (20, 21), (15, 36), (49, 40), (18, 22), (20, 21), (21, 24), (14, 31), and (48, 41) did not visibly change the board state, suggesting either the clicks didn't register or the interaction requires multiple steps. The directional movement actions (UP, UP, UP, UP, RIGHT, LEFT, DOWN, RIGHT, UP, DOWN, LEFT, UP, DOWN, RIGHT, LEFT, UP, DOWN, RIGHT, LEFT, UP, DOWN, RIGHT, LEFT, DOWN, UP) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the blue square (row 20, col 21) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the yellow square (row 20, col 25) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the red part of the L-shaped structure (row 20, col 31) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the red rectangular shape (row 15, col 32) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent directional movement (DOWN) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the blue rectangular shape (row 48, col 40) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent directional movement (RIGHT) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent directional movement (UP) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the gray rectangular block (row 17, col 19) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the green square within the gray rectangular block (row 17, col 17) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the blue square (row 20, col 21) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the yellow square (row 20, col 25) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the red rectangular shape (row 15, col 32) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the blue rectangular shape (row 48, col 40) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the red part of the L-shaped structure (row 20, col 31) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the gray rectangular block (row 17, col 19) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the green square within the gray rectangular block (row 17, col 17) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the yellow square (row 20, col 25) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the blue square (row 20, col 21) also didn't visibly change the board state. The most recent MOUSE click on the red part of the L-shaped structure (row 20, col 31) also didn't visibly change the board state.
- Open questions: ** What specific interaction is needed? Should I click on objects, or is movement the primary mechanism? What constitutes "clearing" this level?
- Plan: ** I have exhausted all individual clicks and simple movements without any visible effect. The level appears to require a different approach. I will try moving the blue square on the left side by pressing RIGHT to see if it moves across the divider or interacts with other objects. This is my final attempt to interact with objects before considering alternative approaches.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 76 actions; a strong score needs about 59 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.
